title: Roadmap & AI
text: This page summarizes planned improvements and future directions for VedicDateTime, based on the package paper, the CRAN package, and ongoing development work.

title: Roadmap & AI
text:
Near-term roadmap

1) Documentation and onboarding

Expand tutorials for common workflows (Panchanga for a place, batch conversion, feature engineering for time-series)
Publish and maintain pkgdown API reference under this website (API (pkgdown)) so function documentation stays accurate and searchable



2) Broader calendrical coverage

Support additional Vedic calendar variants and conversion methods where appropriate
Improve utilities for common research tasks (e.g., working with ranges, repeated conversions, and reproducible “place” definitions)



3) Accuracy, reproducibility, and validation

Expand cross-validation against authoritative sources and other well-established implementations
Increase regression tests for edge cases such as skipped entities and midnight-crossing end times



4) Performance and scalability

Vectorized and parallel workflows for large date ranges
Caching of repeated astronomical computations for faster repeated queries

title: Roadmap & AI
text:
Medium-term roadmap (from the paper’s limitations)
The paper notes areas for future work:

Visualization capabilities for calendar entities
Native R date-time object support (e.g., an S4 class) for Vedic date-time handling

title: Roadmap & AI
text:
AI-assisted directions (future)
The following are future ideas to improve usability and contributor productivity, while keeping computational results grounded in the package’s algorithms and documentation.

1) Documentation assistant (grounded Q&A)

A Q&A layer that answers user questions by searching pkgdown docs + vignettes + the paper, then linking back to the exact functions and help pages.



2) Example and workflow generator

Generate reproducible R snippets for common tasks (e.g., “compute tithi/nakshatra for a location for one year”, “create regressors around a festival window”) using the existing API.



3) Contributor support and triage

Auto-label GitHub issues (bug / doc / enhancement) using issue text
Suggest minimal reproducible examples and missing diagnostics (e.g., location/timezone details)



4) AI-assisted time-series feature engineering templates

Provide templates that convert Gregorian timestamps into Vedic features (tithi, nakshatra, yoga, masa) for modeling pipelines.

title: Roadmap & AI

title: VedicDateTime website (GitHub Pages)
text:
This folder contains a Quarto website intended to be published with GitHub Pages.



Install Quarto: https://quarto.org/
From this folder:

quarto preview



quarto render
This renders the site to docs/ (configured in _quarto.yml).




Commit the website source files (this site/ folder) and the generated docs/ folder to your default branch (e.g., main).
In GitHub repo → Settings → Pages:

Source: Deploy from a branch
Branch: main
Folder: /docs

Save.

The project site URL is:

https://<OWNER>.github.io/<REPO>/

Example:

https://neerajdhanraj.github.io/VedicDateTime/




GitHub Pages redirects to a custom domain if:

Settings → Pages → Custom domain is set, or
a CNAME file exists in the published site root (e.g., docs/CNAME)

To enable a custom domain later:

Set the custom domain in Settings → Pages.
Add a docs/CNAME file with your domain name.
Configure DNS at your domain provider.

title: VedicDateTime
text:
An open-source R package to compute key entities from the Vedic (lunisolar) calendar and convert Gregorian dates (or Julian Day Numbers) into Panchanga components such as tithi, vaara, nakshatra, yoga, and karana.

CRAN: cran.r-project.org/package=VedicDateTime
GitHub: github.com/neerajdhanraj/VedicDateTime
Getting started Cookbook API (pkgdown)

title: VedicDateTime
text:
Package status



CRAN status

title: VedicDateTime
text:
What you get


Panchanga entities
Compute:

tithi
vaara
nakshatra
yoga
karana



Conversions

Gregorian → JD (gregorian_to_jd())
JD → Gregorian (jd_to_gregorian())



Location-aware results
Many computations depend on:

latitude
longitude
timezone offset



Time-series features
Transform timestamps into Vedic features (tithi/nakshatra/yoga/masa) for modeling pipelines.


Documentation

Reference overview
API (pkgdown)
Vignettes

title: VedicDateTime
text:
Quick start
install.packages("VedicDateTime")

library(VedicDateTime)
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)
jd <- gregorian_to_jd(8, 9, 2022)

tithi(jd, place)

title: VedicDateTime
text:
Use cases

Calendar-aware feature engineering for time series
Research workflows requiring lunisolar calendar entities
Reproducible calendrical computations for a specified location

title: FAQ
text: VedicDateTime is an R package that implements core entities from the Vedic (lunisolar) calendar and helps convert Gregorian date-time (or Julian Day Number) into Vedic calendar entities such as tithi, vaara, nakshatra, yoga, and karana.

title: FAQ
text: VedicDateTime is an R package that implements core entities from the Vedic (lunisolar) calendar and helps convert Gregorian date-time (or Julian Day Number) into Vedic calendar entities such as tithi, vaara, nakshatra, yoga, and karana.

title: FAQ
section: Do I need to know astronomy to use it?
text:
Do I need to know astronomy to use it?
No. Most users only provide:

a date (Gregorian or Julian day)
a location (latitude/longitude)
a timezone offset

The package uses established astronomical calculations internally (as described in the paper).

title: FAQ
section: Why do many functions require a “place” (lat/long/timezone)?
text:
Why do many functions require a “place” (lat/long/timezone)?
Because several Vedic calendar entities are defined relative to local sunrise and depend on the observer’s location. Therefore, the same Julian day can correspond to different Panchanga values at different places.

title: FAQ
section: Why do some outputs show times greater than 24:00?
text:
Why do some outputs show times greater than 24:00?
The package examples in the paper note that if an ending time exceeds 24:00, it indicates a time past midnight (i.e., on the next Gregorian day).

title: FAQ
section: Why do I sometimes get two tithis (or a skipped tithi)?
text:
Why do I sometimes get two tithis (or a skipped tithi)?
The paper explains that sometimes two tithis occur within a single civil date and that the next day’s tithi can be checked to detect skipped/leap cases. In such cases, the function may return multiple entity/end-time pairs.

title: FAQ
text:
Is it on CRAN?
Yes:

https://cran.r-project.org/package=VedicDateTime

title: FAQ
text:
Where is the source code?

GitHub: https://github.com/neerajdhanraj/VedicDateTime

title: FAQ
section: How do I report a bug or request a feature?
text:
How do I report a bug or request a feature?
Please open a GitHub Issue:

https://github.com/neerajdhanraj/VedicDateTime/issues

Include:

the exact function call
your input date(s)
place (lat, long, timezone)
your R session info (sessionInfo())

title: FAQ
text:
How accurate are results?
The package uses well-established astronomical computations and is described in a peer-reviewed paper. For strict reproducibility, always record:

package version
timezone / location
input dates and coordinate conventions

title: Cookbook
text: This page provides practical recipes for common tasks with VedicDateTime.

title: Cookbook
section: Recipe 1 — Compute Panchanga for a single date and place
text:
Recipe 1 — Compute Panchanga for a single date and place
library(VedicDateTime)

place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)
jd <- gregorian_to_jd(8, 9, 2022)

tithi(jd, place)
vaara(jd)
nakshatra(jd, place)
yoga(jd, place)
karana(jd, place)

title: Cookbook
section: Recipe 2 — Add Vedic features to a data frame
text:
Recipe 2 — Add Vedic features to a data frame
library(VedicDateTime)

place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

df <- data.frame(
  dd = c(8, 9, 10),
  mm = c(9, 9, 9),
  yyyy = c(2022, 2022, 2022)
)

df$jd <- mapply(gregorian_to_jd, df$dd, df$mm, df$yyyy)

# First element is typically the index; outputs may include end-times too
# (see the function help / pkgdown for details)

df$tithi <- sapply(df$jd, function(j) tithi(j, place)[1])

df$vaara <- sapply(df$jd, function(j) vaara(j))

df$nakshatra <- sapply(df$jd, function(j) nakshatra(j, place)[1])

df$yoga <- sapply(df$jd, function(j) yoga(j, place)[1])

df

title: Cookbook
section: Recipe 3 — Convert numeric indices to names
text:
Recipe 3 — Convert numeric indices to names
library(VedicDateTime)

jd <- 2459778
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

get_vaara_name(jd)
get_tithi_name(jd, place)
get_nakshatra_name(jd, place)
get_yoga_name(jd, place)

title: Cookbook
text:
Recipe 4 — Sunrise/sunset and longitudes
library(VedicDateTime)

place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)
jd <- gregorian_to_jd(8, 9, 2022)

sunrise(jd, place)
sunset(jd, place)

sun_longitude(jd)
moon_longitude(jd)
lunar_phase(jd)

title: Cookbook
section: Recipe 5 — Use Vedic features in a simple model (template)
text:
Recipe 5 — Use Vedic features in a simple model (template)
# Template only: replace y with your time-series response
# df$y <- ...

# model <- lm(y ~ factor(tithi) + factor(nakshatra) + factor(vaara), data = df)
# summary(model)

title: Tutorials & case studies
text: This section collects longer-form explanations, worked examples, and the case studies presented in the VedicDateTime paper.

title: Tutorials & case studies
text:
Core idea
Most computations follow the pattern:

Convert a Gregorian date to a Julian day (gregorian_to_jd) or start from a JD.
Provide a location as place <- c(latitude, longitude, timezone_offset).
Compute one or more Vedic entities (tithi/vaara/nakshatra/yoga/karana) and optionally map indices to names.

title: Tutorials & case studies
text:
Worked examples (from the package README)

Example 1 — tithi (lunar day) for a Gregorian date
library("VedicDateTime")
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

tithi(gregorian_to_jd(08, 09, 2022), place)


Example 2 — vaara (weekday) for a Gregorian date
library("VedicDateTime")

vaara(gregorian_to_jd(08, 09, 2022))


Example 3 — masa (lunar month) for a Gregorian date
library("VedicDateTime")
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

masa(gregorian_to_jd(08, 09, 2022), place)


Example 4 — Gregorian → Julian day
library("VedicDateTime")

gregorian_to_jd(08, 09, 2022) # dd, mm, yyyy

title: Tutorials & case studies
text:
Vignettes
The package includes vignette materials that illustrate computations and workflows.

Tithi vignette (PDF)
Nakshatra vignette (PDF)
Yoga vignette (PDF)

title: Tutorials & case studies
section: Case study 1 (paper) — Calendar seasonality and economic output
text:
Case study 1 (paper) — Calendar seasonality and economic output
The paper highlights that calendars can affect economic and social behavior, and discusses India’s Index of Industrial Production (IIP) as an example.
Key motivations described:

India has multiple regions and festivals from multiple religions.
A single Gregorian calendar representation may be insufficient to model various seasonal components.
During Diwali, consumer consumption can be higher while industrial output is generally lower.

VedicDateTime is used to convert the time axis and analyze calendar seasonality effects.

title: Tutorials & case studies
section: Case study 2 (paper) — Diwali regressors (moving festival effects)
text:
Case study 2 (paper) — Diwali regressors (moving festival effects)
The paper also discusses creating regressors around the Diwali period and comparing industrial production patterns pre and post Diwali.
Motivation:

Moving holidays create shifting impacts in Gregorian time.
Encoding festival timing in a lunisolar calendar can provide stable features for modeling.

title: Tutorials & case studies
text:
Read the paper

DOI: doi.org/10.1007/s11042-023-16553-w
Embedded PDF: see the Publication page

title: About
text:
VedicDateTime is an R package for computing entities from the Vedic (lunisolar) calendar system and converting between Gregorian time (or Julian Day Numbers) and Vedic calendar entities.

CRAN: cran.r-project.org/package=VedicDateTime
GitHub: github.com/neerajdhanraj/VedicDateTime
Paper (open access): doi.org/10.1007/s11042-023-16553-w

title: About
text:
VedicDateTime is an R package for computing entities from the Vedic (lunisolar) calendar system and converting between Gregorian time (or Julian Day Numbers) and Vedic calendar entities.

CRAN: cran.r-project.org/package=VedicDateTime
GitHub: github.com/neerajdhanraj/VedicDateTime
Paper (open access): doi.org/10.1007/s11042-023-16553-w

title: About
text:
Authors (paper)
The package is described in the peer‑reviewed paper authored by:

Neeraj Dhanraj Bokde
Prajwal Kailasnath Patil
Saradindu Sengupta
Manisha Sawant
Andrés E. Feijóo‑Lorenzo

title: About
text:
Maintainer

Neeraj Dhanraj Bokde (Maintainer)
Email: neerajdhanraj@gmail.com

title: About
text:
Contributors
Additional contributions are tracked in the GitHub repository:

github.com/neerajdhanraj/VedicDateTime

title: About
text:
Scope
VedicDateTime implements the five key Panchanga entities:

tithi
vaara
nakshatra
yoga
karana

It also provides supporting functions for related entities and astronomical calculations, including:

sunrise/sunset, moonrise/moonset
solar/lunar longitudes
rashi, lagna, masa, ritu, samvatsara
Gregorian ↔︎ Julian day conversions

title: About
text:
Implementation notes (paper summary)
The paper describes algorithmic design choices including:

computations anchored to local sunrise (location-dependent)
Lahiri Ayanamsa for sidereal corrections
use of Swiss Ephemeris (via R dependencies) for astronomical computations
inverse Lagrange interpolation (with 0.25-day offsets) to compute end times

title: About
text:
License
VedicDateTime is distributed under GPL-3.

title: About
text:
Funding acknowledgement
Early development of this package was funded through Google Summer of Code (GSoC) in multiple years.

title: About
section: Limitations & future scope (as stated in the paper)
text:
Limitations & future scope (as stated in the paper)
The paper notes future improvements such as:

adding visualization capabilities for calendar entities
adding native R date-time object support (e.g., S4 classes) for Vedic date-time handling

title: Accuracy & conventions
text: This page explains key conventions for using VedicDateTime correctly and reproducibly.

title: Accuracy & conventions
text:
Location matters
Many entities in the Vedic calendar are computed relative to local sunrise and depend on the observer’s location.
This is why several functions take a place vector:
place <- c(latitude, longitude, timezone_offset)
Two different places can yield different values (or different end times) for the same civil date.

title: Accuracy & conventions
text:
Timezone convention
The examples use a numeric timezone offset (e.g., +5.5). Always keep timezone consistent with the location.

title: Accuracy & conventions
text:
Julian Day Number (JD)
Many computations start from a Julian Day Number.

Use gregorian_to_jd(dd, mm, yyyy) when starting from a Gregorian date.
If your workflow already uses JD, you can directly call Panchanga functions.

title: Accuracy & conventions
text:
Interpreting output vectors
Several functions return numeric vectors that include:

an index (which entity)
an end time represented as hour minute second

Example pattern:
x <- tithi(jd, place)

# often:
# x[1] = tithi index
# x[2:4] = end time (h, m, s)

Times beyond 24:00
If the end time exceeds 24:00, it indicates the entity ends after midnight (i.e., on the next civil day).

title: Accuracy & conventions
text:
Reproducibility checklist
For reproducible results, record:

package version
date/time convention (Gregorian inputs vs JD)
place vector used (latitude, longitude, timezone_offset)

title: Accuracy & conventions
text:
Accuracy and validation
VedicDateTime uses established astronomical computations (via dependencies such as Swiss Ephemeris bindings). For detailed algorithm and background, see:

API docs: API (pkgdown)
CRAN: cran.r-project.org/package=VedicDateTime

title: Getting started
text:
VedicDateTime is available on CRAN:

https://cran.r-project.org/package=VedicDateTime

Install it in R:
install.packages("VedicDateTime")
Load the package:
library(VedicDateTime)



If you want the latest development version:
# install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("neerajdhanraj/VedicDateTime")

title: Getting started
text:
VedicDateTime is available on CRAN:

https://cran.r-project.org/package=VedicDateTime

Install it in R:
install.packages("VedicDateTime")
Load the package:
library(VedicDateTime)



If you want the latest development version:
# install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("neerajdhanraj/VedicDateTime")

title: Getting started
section: The key idea: compute Vedic entities for a time + place
text:
The key idea: compute Vedic entities for a time + place
Many Vedic calendar computations depend on location (because several entities are defined relative to local sunrise). In the package paper, most core functions use:

jd: a Julian Day Number (JD)
place: a numeric vector with latitude, longitude, timezone offset

Example place vector shown in the paper:
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

title: Getting started
section: Step 1 — Start from a Julian Day Number
text:
Step 1 — Start from a Julian Day Number

Option A: you already have a JD
If your analysis already uses Julian days, you can directly call the functions.


Option B: convert a Gregorian date to JD
The paper describes gregorian_to_jd() as the conversion helper. The paper also notes it converts to Julian day number at 00:00 UTC.
Example pattern (shown in the paper):
jd <- gregorian_to_jd(17, 6, 2022)

Tip: Always record the timezone convention you are using in your analysis.

title: Getting started
text:
Step 2 — Compute Panchanga (five arms)
The Panchanga consists of: tithi, vaara, nakshatra, yoga, karana.

2.1 tithi()
What the paper says:

The tithi at sunrise is determined by the difference between lunar and solar longitudes divided by 12.
The fractional part indicates remaining degrees to the next tithi.
Ending time is computed using Inverse Lagrange interpolation with longitudes at 0.25 day intervals from sunrise.

Example (paper Listing):
jd <- 2459778
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

tithi(jd, place)
#> [1] 20 20 55 35
Interpretation (paper):

20 is the tithi index (30 tithis total). The paper’s example indicates this corresponds to Krishna Paksha Panchami.
20 55 35 indicates end time (20h 55m 35s).

Sometimes, two tithis can occur on a given date, and in special cases a tithi can be skipped; the paper describes how such cases are handled.
To get a name from a number:
# get_tithi_name(jd, place)


2.2 vaara()
Vaara is the weekday (7 in a week). The paper shows:
vaara(2459778)
#> [1] 1

get_vaara_name(2459778)
#> [1] "Ravivar"

get_vaara_name(gregorian_to_jd(6, 8, 2022))
#> [1] "Shanivar"


2.3 nakshatra()
From the paper, nakshatra() is computed using the moon’s position:

compute the moon’s sidereal (Nirayana) longitude by subtracting Ayanamsa from tropical longitude
the paper states the package uses Lahiri Ayanamsa
sunrise is obtained using Swiss Ephemeris
end times use Inverse Lagrange interpolation with 0.25 day offsets

Example (paper Listing):
nakshatra(2459778, c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5))
#> [1] 25 24 24  1
The paper explains: 25 corresponds to Purvabhadrapada, and 24 24 1 indicates end time (24:24:01 → after midnight).


2.4 yoga()
The paper notes 27 Yogas (each 13°20′ of arc). Yoga is the sum of the sidereal longitudes of the sun and moon.
Example (paper Listing):
yoga(2459778, c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5))
#> [1]  5 27 26 12


2.5 karana()
The paper describes karana as half of a tithi (moon traverses 6° relative to the sun). There are 60 half‑tithis in a lunar month.
Example (paper Listing):
karana(2459778, c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5))
#> [1] 39 40

get_karana_name(39, 40)
#> [1] "Kaulava-Taitila"

title: Getting started
text:
Step 3 — Explore additional functions
The paper lists additional functions (see Reference), including:

sunrise(), sunset(), moonrise(), moonset()
sun_longitude(), moon_longitude()
rashi(), lagna(), masa(), ritu(), samvatsara()

title: Getting started
text:
Practical tips

Record inputs for reproducibility
For reproducible results, always record:

package version
your timezone convention
latitude/longitude/timezone used



Understand times beyond 24:00
The paper notes: if output timings exceed 24:00, they indicate a time past midnight (next day).

title: Getting started
text:

title: Publication
text:
The VedicDateTime package is described in the following peer‑reviewed paper:
VedicDateTime: An R package to implement Vedic calendar system
Neeraj Dhanraj Bokde, Prajwal Kailasnath Patil, Saradindu Sengupta, Manisha Sawant, Andrés E. Feijóo‑Lorenzo
Multimedia Tools and Applications (2023)

Springer page: link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11042-023-16553-w
DOI: doi.org/10.1007/s11042-023-16553-w

title: Reference
text: This page is a practical guide to VedicDateTime functions and how to use them. For the complete function index with argument tables, see the pkgdown site:

title: Reference
text:
Core inputs
Many functions work with:

jd: Julian Day Number
place: numeric vector c(latitude, longitude, timezone_offset)

Example:
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

title: Reference
text:
Panchanga (five arms)

tithi()
Compute the tithi (lunar day) for a given JD and place.
library(VedicDateTime)
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

tithi(gregorian_to_jd(8, 9, 2022), place)
Related:

tithis()
get_tithi_name()



vaara()
Compute the weekday.
library(VedicDateTime)

vaara(gregorian_to_jd(8, 9, 2022))
Related:

vaaras()
get_vaara_name()



nakshatra()
Compute nakshatra for a JD and place.
library(VedicDateTime)
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

nakshatra(gregorian_to_jd(8, 9, 2022), place)
Related:

nakshatras()
get_nakshatra_name()



yoga()
Compute yoga for a JD and place.
library(VedicDateTime)
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

yoga(gregorian_to_jd(8, 9, 2022), place)
Related:

yogas()
get_yoga_name()



karana()
Compute karana (half‑tithi) for a JD and place.
library(VedicDateTime)
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

karana(gregorian_to_jd(8, 9, 2022), place)
Related:

karanas()
get_karana_name()

title: Reference
text:
Conversions

gregorian_to_jd() — Gregorian date → Julian day
jd_to_gregorian() — Julian day → Gregorian date

title: Reference
text:

moonrise(), moonset()
sun_longitude(), moon_longitude()
lunar_phase(), new_moon()

text:

rashi() / get_rashi_name()
lagna() / get_lagna_name()
masa() / get_masa_name()
ritu() / get_ritu_name()
samvatsara() / get_samvatsara_name()
